comments:
Summary:
This paper studies the Euclidean projection of an arbitrary vector onto the intersection of an s-sparsity constraint and either the positive simplex (nonnegative entries summing to λ) or the general simplex (entries summing to λ, possibly with negatives). The authors propose (i) a simple greedy selector followed by a standard simplex projection (GSSP) and prove it is exact for the positive simplex case, and (ii) a relaxation-based iterative scheme (RSSP) for the general simplex that searches over shifted projections (parameterized by τ) and finds the correct sparsity support in almost all cases; they provide monotonicity and piecewise-linear structure results for the search map and give an approximation guarantee when exact equality cannot be found. Experiments include synthetic benchmarks and applications to portfolio optimization and kernel density estimation, with comparisons to CPLEX and a proximal alternating minimization method.

Strengths:
- The positive-simplex result (GSSP) is neat and practically useful: the proof is straightforward and leverages simple combinatorial/thresholding properties; the algorithm is extremely cheap (hard-threshold then simplex projection restricted to support).
- The analysis for RSSP (monotonicity of C(τ), turning points, and a bisection-based search) is plausible and gives a practical algorithm with modest complexity and good empirical performance.
- The experimental section demonstrates practical utility in portfolio selection and density estimation; comparisons with CPLEX show large speedups with matching solution quality for tested regimes.
- The paper addresses a practically important class of constrained sparse problems where the usual ℓ1-relaxation is not suitable.

Weaknesses and concerns:
- The general-simplex algorithm (RSSP) is not proven to be exact in all cases; Theorem 2 states exactness for all λ except a set of finite measure and leaves some cases handled only by approximation guarantees or conjecture. The paper should more precisely characterize the exceptional set (Λ), give intuition about when such degeneracies occur in practice, and, if possible, provide exact conditions under which RSSP fails.
- Several parts of the theoretical development rely on non-degeneracy assumptions (e.g., distinct entries of w, or avoiding turning points). While the authors mention tie-breaking via lexicographic rules, more explicit treatment of ties/degeneracies would strengthen the exposition and avoid hidden corner cases.
- Lemma 4 / approximation guarantee is useful but the bound is somewhat opaque; it would help to give simpler corollaries (e.g., bounds on objective gap or relative error under natural conditions) and discuss tightness.
- Experimental details are sometimes sparse: parameter settings for competing solvers (CPLEX configuration, PAMP tuning), number of random trials, seeds, and runtimes relative to machine hardware are not fully specified in the provided text. Releasing code or including more reproducibility details would improve the empirical claims.
- Related work: while the paper mentions a few related papers, a more complete discussion relating these projection operators to other sparse projection or combinatorial projection literatures (and to techniques for sparse-constrained quadratic programming) would be helpful to locate the contribution.

Minor points / clarity:
- Complexity claims differ slightly across sections (O(p max(s, log p)) for hard-thresholding, later O(ps log^2 s) vs O(ps log2(s))); a clear final summary of computational complexity for both algorithms would help readers.
- Pseudocode for RSSP is given at a high level; including an explicit bisection stopping criteria and numerical tolerances used in experiments would be useful.

Recommendations for improvement:
- Provide a more explicit and complete characterization of the exceptional set Λ for which exactness is not guaranteed; if a full characterization is hard, provide practical diagnostic checks and remedies for these cases.
- Expand experimental details for reproducibility (solver settings, platforms, seeds) and, if possible, publish code.
- Add a short subsection explicitly handling ties / degenerate w and discuss numerical robustness of the bisection when C(τ) is multi-valued.
- Include some additional theoretical corollaries that translate the F(S) guarantee into more interpretable error measures (objective gap or norm gap) under reasonable assumptions.

Overall evaluation:
This is a well-motivated and solid contribution: the exact greedy projector for the positive simplex is a useful and provably-correct result, and the RSSP approach for the general simplex is a practical and well-justified heuristic with attractive empirical performance and some theoretical backing. The paper strikes a good balance of theory, algorithms, and experiments. With the suggested clarifications and additional exposition on degenerate cases and experimental details, it will be a valuable reference for practitioners needing sparse simplex projections.
